MEANINGFUL_COMPARISON.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper compares against multiple and relevant baselines (3-gram LM, RNNLM, combined LM, and established LM-based selection methods of Moore & Lewis and Axelrod et al.), uses the same corpora and BLEU with bootstrap resampling for significance, and discusses related work thoroughly. However, the summary gives no evidence of multiple independent runs or hyperparameter reporting (I penalize lack of experimental/statistical detail), and it is unclear whether the authors explicitly acknowledge cases where prior methods perform comparably; analysis of why the CNN outperforms baselines also appears limited. Overall the empirical comparisons are strong but missing a few elements of rigor and explanatory analysis expected for full confidence. | B(4): The paper compares to appropriate and strong baselines (3-gram LM, RNNLM, combined LM) and state-of-the-art LM-based selection methods (Moore & Lewis; Axelrod et al.), uses the same datasets and BLEU with significance testing, and reports clear BLEU improvements. However, reproducibility details (hyperparameters, code) are not reported and the paper provides limited analysis/ablations on why the CNN yields gains versus the LM baselines; it does acknowledge limitations and discusses related work thoroughly. Given these minor omissions, the comparisons are good but not fully comprehensive.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper compares against multiple and relevant baselines (3-gram LM, RNNLM, combined LM, and established LM-based selection methods of Moore & Lewis and Axelrod et al.), uses the same corpora and BLEU with bootstrap resampling for significance, and discusses related work thoroughly. However, the summary gives no evidence of multiple independent runs or hyperparameter reporting (I penalize lack of experimental/statistical detail), and it is unclear whether the authors explicitly acknowledge cases where prior methods perform comparably; analysis of why the CNN outperforms baselines also appears limited. Overall the empirical comparisons are strong but missing a few elements of rigor and explanatory analysis expected for full confidence.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper compares to appropriate and strong baselines (3-gram LM, RNNLM, combined LM) and state-of-the-art LM-based selection methods (Moore & Lewis; Axelrod et al.), uses the same datasets and BLEU with significance testing, and reports clear BLEU improvements. However, reproducibility details (hyperparameters, code) are not reported and the paper provides limited analysis/ablations on why the CNN yields gains versus the LM baselines; it does acknowledge limitations and discusses related work thoroughly. Given these minor omissions, the comparisons are good but not fully comprehensive.

APPROPRIATENESS.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The topic—domain adaptation and data selection for SMT using semi-supervised CNNs—is squarely within top-tier NLP/ML venues and targets the right audience given comparisons to strong baselines (ngram, rnnlm, comblm, Moore & Lewis, Axelrod et al.) and use of BLEU with bootstrap resampling. However, from a theoretical-purist perspective the paper is primarily empirical with no learning-theoretic justification or analysis of why the CNN selection works, and important experimental details (hyperparameters, code) are not reported, which weakens reproducibility. Overall it is a good fit but would benefit from stronger theoretical motivation and fuller experimental rigor. | B(4): This paper is a good fit for a top NLP/ML venue: it addresses a practical, widely-relevant problem (domain adaptation/data selection for MT), provides thorough empirical comparisons against strong LM-based baselines (Moore & Lewis, Axelrod et al., RNNLM) and reports meaningful BLEU gains (up to 3.1) across multiple datasets including SMS/TED/WMT. From a pragmatic empirical perspective the emphasis on very small in-domain sets (≤100 sentences) and detailed evaluation is compelling; however, the absence of reported hyperparameters and no mention of released code weakens reproducibility and prevents it from being a perfect fit without minor scope concerns.

reviewer_a.justification: The topic—domain adaptation and data selection for SMT using semi-supervised CNNs—is squarely within top-tier NLP/ML venues and targets the right audience given comparisons to strong baselines (ngram, rnnlm, comblm, Moore & Lewis, Axelrod et al.) and use of BLEU with bootstrap resampling. However, from a theoretical-purist perspective the paper is primarily empirical with no learning-theoretic justification or analysis of why the CNN selection works, and important experimental details (hyperparameters, code) are not reported, which weakens reproducibility. Overall it is a good fit but would benefit from stronger theoretical motivation and fuller experimental rigor.

reviewer_b.justification: This paper is a good fit for a top NLP/ML venue: it addresses a practical, widely-relevant problem (domain adaptation/data selection for MT), provides thorough empirical comparisons against strong LM-based baselines (Moore & Lewis, Axelrod et al., RNNLM) and reports meaningful BLEU gains (up to 3.1) across multiple datasets including SMS/TED/WMT. From a pragmatic empirical perspective the emphasis on very small in-domain sets (≤100 sentences) and detailed evaluation is compelling; however, the absence of reported hyperparameters and no mention of released code weakens reproducibility and prevents it from being a perfect fit without minor scope concerns.

CLARITY.justification: [Consensus avg=4] A(4): The paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early (proposing a semi‑supervised CNN for in‑domain bilingual selection and demonstrating effectiveness with as few as 100 sentences). Presentation of experiments and baselines is thorough (multiple datasets, strong LM and recent selection baselines, BLEU with bootstrap significance), which aids clarity. However, some presentation details that matter for reproducibility and interpretability are missing from the summary (no hyperparameter reporting, code not mentioned), and the summary does not confirm whether notation and all figures/tables are consistently defined — these omissions prevent a top score from my theoretical‑purist viewpoint. | B(4): The paper is well organized with a clear progression (introduction -> method -> experiments -> conclusion) and the main contributions are stated early (semi‑supervised CNN for data selection, effectiveness with as few as 100 sentences). Notation and presentation are generally consistent and the writing quality is good, with thorough related work; figures/tables and examples appear to be informative for experimental claims (BLEU improvements up to 3.1). A minor presentation shortcoming is the lack of reported hyperparameters and code availability, which hinders reproducibility even though the methodological description is clear.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper is well organized and the main contributions are stated early (proposing a semi‑supervised CNN for in‑domain bilingual selection and demonstrating effectiveness with as few as 100 sentences). Presentation of experiments and baselines is thorough (multiple datasets, strong LM and recent selection baselines, BLEU with bootstrap significance), which aids clarity. However, some presentation details that matter for reproducibility and interpretability are missing from the summary (no hyperparameter reporting, code not mentioned), and the summary does not confirm whether notation and all figures/tables are consistently defined — these omissions prevent a top score from my theoretical‑purist viewpoint.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper is well organized with a clear progression (introduction -> method -> experiments -> conclusion) and the main contributions are stated early (semi‑supervised CNN for data selection, effectiveness with as few as 100 sentences). Notation and presentation are generally consistent and the writing quality is good, with thorough related work; figures/tables and examples appear to be informative for experimental claims (BLEU improvements up to 3.1). A minor presentation shortcoming is the lack of reported hyperparameters and code availability, which hinders reproducibility even though the methodological description is clear.

ORIGINALITY.justification: [Consensus avg=3] A(3): The paper applies a semi-supervised convolutional neural network to the well-studied problem of bilingual data selection for SMT and shows empirical gains (up to 3.1 BLEU) with as few as 100 in‑domain sentences versus LM‑based baselines (Moore & Lewis, Axelrod et al.). While the specific design (separate source/target scoring with summed scores) is a practical and evaluated instantiation, it is primarily an application of existing CNN classification ideas to data selection rather than a fundamentally new theoretical method. From my perspective, the contribution is a moderate, empirically driven extension that is clearly differentiated from prior LM approaches but lacks deeper theoretical justification or novel algorithmic guarantees, so it reads as incremental rather than highly original. | B(3): The paper applies a semi-supervised convolutional classifier to the well-studied problem of bilingual data selection and shows it works with very small (≈100 sentence) in-domain seeds, which is a practically valuable twist. Scoring source and target separately and summing scores is a simple but concrete methodological difference from prior LM-based selection (Moore & Lewis; Axelrod et al.). However, the core problem and the idea of using classifiers or neural models for selection are not fundamentally new, so the contribution reads as an incremental yet useful extension rather than a highly original breakthrough (despite empirical gains up to 3.1 BLEU).

reviewer_a.justification: The paper applies a semi-supervised convolutional neural network to the well-studied problem of bilingual data selection for SMT and shows empirical gains (up to 3.1 BLEU) with as few as 100 in‑domain sentences versus LM‑based baselines (Moore & Lewis, Axelrod et al.). While the specific design (separate source/target scoring with summed scores) is a practical and evaluated instantiation, it is primarily an application of existing CNN classification ideas to data selection rather than a fundamentally new theoretical method. From my perspective, the contribution is a moderate, empirically driven extension that is clearly differentiated from prior LM approaches but lacks deeper theoretical justification or novel algorithmic guarantees, so it reads as incremental rather than highly original.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper applies a semi-supervised convolutional classifier to the well-studied problem of bilingual data selection and shows it works with very small (≈100 sentence) in-domain seeds, which is a practically valuable twist. Scoring source and target separately and summing scores is a simple but concrete methodological difference from prior LM-based selection (Moore & Lewis; Axelrod et al.). However, the core problem and the idea of using classifiers or neural models for selection are not fundamentally new, so the contribution reads as an incremental yet useful extension rather than a highly original breakthrough (despite empirical gains up to 3.1 BLEU).

REPLICABILITY.justification: Both reviewers agree that publicly available corpora and standard BLEU evaluation are used and the high-level method is described, which aids reproducibility. However, key details (model architecture, hyperparameters, preprocessing, number of runs/seeds) and code release are missing, requiring substantial implementation choices and tuning. Given these gaps but available data and clear evaluation, reproduction is possible but nontrivial, so a middling score of 3 is appropriate.

reviewer_a.justification: Important training details and hyperparameters are not reported, and the paper does not mention code release, making exact reimplementation unlikely. Although the paper lists public corpora (WMT, TED, LDC) and reports BLEU with bootstrap resampling, the data preprocessing pipeline and model configuration (e.g., CNN architecture, embeddings, optimization, number of runs/seeds) are unspecified. Given these omissions, reproducing the exact results would be difficult despite reasonably described methodology and available source corpora.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper uses public corpora (LDC, TED, WMT) and standard evaluation (BLEU with bootstrap resampling) which helps reproducibility, and the high-level method (semi-supervised CNN scoring source/target and selecting top pairs) is described. However, key details are missing: hyperparameters and model configurations are not reported, preprocessing steps are not clearly specified, and code is not provided, making reimplementation nontrivial. Given these gaps but available datasets and clear evaluation, reproduction is possible but would require substantial implementation choices and tuning.

IMPACT.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper addresses an important practical problem and demonstrates tangible BLEU improvements with very small in‑domain data, making it valuable for practitioners. The impact is tempered by limited theoretical analysis, reproducibility details, and scope confined to SMT and non‑online settings, so while not transformative, the work is likely to influence low‑resource domain adaptation approaches and thus merits a strong (4) impact score.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper tackles an important practical problem—data selection for SMT with only a tiny amount of in‑domain data—and presents an empirical recipe (semi‑supervised CNN scoring source and target separately) that yields measurable BLEU gains (up to 3.1) and is compared against reasonable LM baselines with bootstrap significance testing. However, from my theoretical‑purist perspective the contribution is largely empirical and lacks learning‑theoretic analysis or interpretability of why the CNN works, and the authors themselves note limitations for large target corpora and online adaptation. Given solid empirical gains but limited theoretical grounding and scope, the likely impact is moderate: useful to practitioners but unlikely to fundamentally change how researchers think about domain adaptation.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper tackles a practical and important problem—data selection for domain adaptation with extremely small in-domain bilingual samples—and demonstrates tangible BLEU improvements (up to 3.1) over strong LM-based baselines, which is exactly the sort of empirical gain practitioners care about. Its claim of effectiveness with as few as ~100 sentences and evaluations across varied corpora (LDC, TED, WMT; sms/tweet/facebook dev/test) make it likely to change how people approach low-resource domain adaptation. Impact is tempered by limited reproducibility details (no hyperparameters/code mentioned) and focus on SMT (not shown for large-scale or online settings), so I judge it a strong but not transformative contribution.

SUBSTANCE.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper provides strong multi-domain empirical evidence against several strong baselines with significance testing, supporting the main effectiveness claim. They also both note missing reproducibility details (hyperparameters/code) and a lack of ablation/sensitivity analyses, which limits interpretability and robustness. Given the solid experimental gains but important omitted controls, a slightly above-mid score reflects usefulness tempered by these shortcomings.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper evaluates on multiple realistic corpora (LDC, TED, WMT variants) and compares against several strong LM baselines (ngram, rnnlm, comblm, Moore & Lewis, Axelrod), and uses bootstrap resampling for significance — evidence sufficient to support empirical claims at a basic level. However, as a theoretical purist I note the absence of any learning-theoretic justification or mechanistic analysis explaining why the semi-supervised CNN works, no reported hyperparameters or reproducibility details, and no mention of ablations or sensitivity analyses (e.g., number of negative samples, CNN architecture choices, or variability across runs). The paper does acknowledge some limitations (domain provenance, scaling to large corpora), but overall important experimental controls and analyses that would explain and reliably validate the method are missing.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper provides solid, multi-domain empirical evidence (LDC SMS, TED, WMT corpora) and compares against strong baselines (Moore-Lewis, Axelrod, RNNLM, combined LMs) with significance testing and reported BLEU gains up to 3.1, which supports the main claim of effectiveness with very small in-domain sets. However, key reproducibility details are missing (hyperparameters and no code mentioned), and the paper lacks clear ablation/sensitivity analyses for design choices (e.g., CNN architecture, negative sampling, selection thresholds). Related work is treated thoroughly and limitations are acknowledged, so overall the experiments are strong but leave important practical details and robustness checks unexamined.

SOUNDNESS_CORRECTNESS.justification: Both reviewers agree the paper makes clear empirical claims and provides solid experimental comparisons with significance testing, supporting its main technical contribution. However, reproducibility and interpretability are weakened by missing hyperparameter details, lack of multiple-run reporting, and no code release; these are important but do not fatally undermine the empirical evidence. Given the strong results and sound experimental design but notable reporting omissions, a score of 4 is appropriate.

reviewer_a.justification: The paper states clear empirical claims and presents experiments comparing against several reasonable LM-based baselines (ngram, rnnlm, comblm, and prior selection methods) with BLEU gains and bootstrap resampling for significance, which supports the main empirical claim. However, from the provided summary there is no theoretical analysis or learning-theoretic justification for why a semi-supervised CNN should reliably select in-domain bilingual pairs, hyperparameters are not reported, and it is unclear whether results reflect multiple independent runs or potential tuning/selection bias. These omissions create methodological and interpretability concerns that weaken confidence in the generality of the claims despite the positive empirical evidence.

reviewer_b.justification: The paper's main claims are clearly stated and supported by empirical evidence: semi-supervised CNN selection yields up to +3.1 BLEU and is reported to work with very small in-domain sets (as few as 100 sentences), compared against strong LM-based baselines (Moore & Lewis, Axelrod) with bootstrap significance testing. There are no complex derivations to verify and the method is described plausibly (scoring source/target separately and summing), but reproducibility is weakened by missing hyperparameter details and no code release. Given strong empirical comparisons and appropriate statistical testing, the technical contribution appears sound though minor reporting omissions prevent a flawless (5) rating.

main_claims: Proposes using a semi-supervised convolutional neural network to select in-domain bilingual training data for statistical machine translation (SMT).
The semi-supervised CNN approach is effective even when only a tiny amount of in-domain data is available (as few as 100 sentences).
The method outperforms state-of-the-art language-model-based data selection methods, improving BLEU up to 3.1 on some tasks.
The CNN selects sentence pairs by scoring source and target separately and selecting pairs based on the sum of source and target in-domain scores.
_summary.methodology: Train a semi-supervised CNN classifier using the small in-domain set as positive samples and randomly sampled general-domain sentences as negatives (one-hot or embedding-based inputs); apply the trained CNN to score all general-domain sentence pairs (scoring source and target separately) and select top-scoring pairs (by summed score) to train an adapted SMT system.
datasets: WMT (Europarl, web crawl, news-commentary, UN proceedings)
Dev/test domains: sms, tweets, Facebook posts
baselines: LM-based selection methods (Moore and Lewis; Axelrod et al.)
limitations: Domain adaptation with only a tiny amount of in-domain data is a hard problem (stated as a challenge).
The experiments did not consider domain provenance of the training data (they did not use domain labels for training data).
Future work is needed to apply the approach for large target-language corpora (e.g., Gigaword) and online dynamic adaptation, implying current work does not cover these scenarios.
_summary.topic_area: Machine Translation - domain adaptation and data selection

reviewer_a.name: Theoretical Purist
reviewer_a.background: Associate professor in machine learning and NLP with a strong track record in theoretical analysis of learning algorithms and model generalization.
traits: Penalizes papers that present mainly empirical results without theoretical justification or analysis (e.g., no learning-theoretic argument or explanation of model behavior).
Penalizes experimental evaluations that lack statistical rigor: deducts points if no significance testing or if results are reported from fewer than 3 independent runs.
Penalizes papers that compare against fewer than three strong baselines, especially if baselines omit recent or widely-adopted approaches.
Prefers methods with clear guarantees or interpretable mechanisms; will downgrade black-box architectures used without probing or analysis.
reviewer_a.reviewing_style: Emphasizes theoretical grounding and rigorous, well-controlled experiments; skeptical of incremental empirical-only contributions unless they include analysis explaining why they work.

reviewer_b.name: Pragmatic Empiricist
reviewer_b.background: Senior research scientist in machine translation and domain adaptation with extensive experience deploying MT systems in industry and evaluating practical gains.
traits: Rewards papers demonstrating clear, reproducible empirical improvements on practical metrics (e.g., BLEU gains >1 point) and over strong baselines.
Gives positive credit for methods that are sample-efficient or work with very small amounts of in-domain data (explicitly favors claims of effectiveness with ≤100 sentences).

Values thorough empirical comparisons and ablation studies; will upgrade papers that include comparisons to state-of-the-art data selection and domain adaptation techniques and that show robustness across multiple tasks/languages.
Penalizes lack of reproducibility details (e.g., missing hyperparameters or training data descriptions), but less concerned about formal theoretical analysis if empirical evidence is strong.
reviewer_b.reviewing_style: Focuses on practical impact and empirical rigor: judges contributions by whether they yield tangible improvements in real-world or benchmark settings and whether experiments are reproducible and comprehensive.
